note_data.title: Reactions of organic compounds
keyPoints: Filtration separates insoluble solid from liquid - liquid particles small enough to go through filter paper pores, solid particles too large

Crystallization obtains solid from solid & liquid solution - process involves dissolving solid in solvent, heating to evaporate solvent, producing hot saturated solution, then crystals of pure solid form on cooling
Distillation obtains pure liquid from solution - solution boils, pure liquid turns to vapour, vapour cooled by condenser and changes back to liquid called 'Distillate'
Fractional Distillation separates 2 liquids which are mixed (miscible) - liquid with lowest BP comes out first at top of fractionating column
Chromatography separates mixture of organic compounds (e.g. colour dyes)
In filtration: solid obtained is called 'Residue' and liquid obtained is called 'Filtrate'
Distillation is possible because pure liquid has low BP and changes to gas easily, while solid does not boil (high BP) so remains in flask
A saturated solution means no more solid can dissolve in it
The constant temperature during distillation process equals the boiling point of pure liquid

definitions.definition: Obtaining solid from Solid & Liquid solution OR Formation of crystals from a cooling liquid/Saturated solution

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the products only. No equations or explanations needed.
Give the reaction + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Include state symbols and balance correctly for full marks

modelAnswer.question: Ethanol can be oxidised to form ethanoic acid. Name the oxidising agent used and state the colour change observed during this reaction.
modelAnswer.answer: Oxidising agent: acidified potassium dichromate(VI) or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄. Colour change: orange to green.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: orange to green colour change

commonMistakes: Confusing which separation method to use for different types of mixtures
Not understanding that crystallization is always the method to obtain crystals from solutions
Mixing up the terms 'residue' and 'filtrate' in filtration
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystallization'
Remember that mixtures are impure substances that need purification methods
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Structural formula of ester showing R-COO-R' arrangement with double bonded oxygen on carbon and single bonded oxygen linking to alkyl group
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to draw a fully labelled diagram showing hydrogen bonding between water and methanol, requiring partial charges and lone pairs to be shown
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — students had to identify which carboxylic acid structure would react with ethanol to produce a sweet-smelling ester
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q6 (5 marks) — candidates needed to identify an optical isomer of C₅H₁₁Cl that gives only 2 alkenes with NaOH/ethanol and explain the stereochemistry
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — students had to explain vapour pressure in terms of gas-liquid equilibrium for methanol

workedExample.question: Compound X has molecular formula C₅H₁₁Cl and exists as 2 optical isomers. When treated with NaOH in ethanol, it gives only 2 different alkenes. Draw the structure of compound X and explain why it shows optical isomerism. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that for optical isomerism, there must be a chiral carbon (carbon bonded to 4 different groups)
Step 2: Consider that elimination gives only 2 alkenes, so the Cl must be on a secondary carbon
Step 3: Draw structure: CH₃-CH₂-CHCl-CH(CH₃)₂ where the carbon bearing Cl is chiral
Step 4: Label the chiral centre with an asterisk (*)
Step 5: Explain that the chiral carbon is bonded to H, Cl, CH₂CH₃, and CH(CH₃)₂ - four different groups
workedExample.answer: Structure: CH₃-CH₂-CHCl*-CH(CH₃)₂. The compound shows optical isomerism because it contains a chiral carbon atom (marked with *) that is bonded to four different groups: H, Cl, ethyl group, and isopropyl group. This gives rise to two non-superimposable mirror images.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct structure, 1 mark for identifying chiral centre, 2 marks for naming the four different groups, 2 marks for explaining optical isomerism in terms of non-superimposable mirror images

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to show partial charges (δ+ and δ-) when drawing hydrogen bonds' — always include partial charges and lone pairs when drawing intermolecular hydrogen bonding

Examiner Report 2024: 'Students often confused elimination and substitution reactions of haloalkanes' — remember that NaOH in ethanol gives elimination (alkenes) while NaOH in water gives substitution (alcohols)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently could not identify chiral centres correctly' — a chiral carbon must be bonded to exactly four different atoms or groups, mark it clearly with an asterisk in your answer
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw hydrogen bonding between molecules showing: (1) the molecules with correct structural formulas, (2) partial charges δ+ on hydrogen and δ- on the electronegative atom, (3) dotted line representing the hydrogen bond between lone pair on electronegative atom and hydrogen, (4) bond angle approximately 180° for strongest hydrogen bond. Example: H₂O···H-O-CH₃ with all charges and lone pairs clearly marked.
note_data.quickRecap: Separation methods depend on the type of mixture: filtration for insoluble solid and liquid, crystallization to obtain solid from solution, distillation to obtain pure liquid from solution, and fractional distillation for miscible liquids. Each method works based on different physical properties like particle size, boiling points, or solubility. The choice of separation technique depends on whether you want to recover the solid or liquid component.
